Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like we’ve got a cushion and we understand what we actually have, not just a bunch of statements.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year right now, roughly?
Client 1: It’s pension income, joint. The annual amount is 104800.
Advisor: Thanks. And on the spending side, about what do you two spend per month all-in?
Client 2: We try to keep it steady. Total monthly expenses are 4000.

Client 1: It’s pension income, joint. The annual amount is 104800.
Advisor: Thanks. And on the spending side, about what do you two spend per month all-in?
Client 2: We try to keep it steady. Total monthly expenses are 4000.
Advisor: Okay. Any regular debt payments each month—mortgage, car loans, or balances you’re carrying?
Client 1: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like we’ve got a cushion and we understand what we actually have, not just a bunch of statements.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year right now, roughly?
Client 1: It’s pension income, joint. The annual amount is 104800.
Advisor: Thanks. And on the spending side, about what do you two spend per month all-in?
Client 2: We try to keep it steady. Total monthly expenses are 4000.

Client 2: Uh, it’s… a lot of accounts. I don’t know the exact total offhand.
Advisor: No problem—what’s the combined total you usually use when you’re thinking about it?
Client 1: The investable assets total is 692300.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s walk through the main accounts so I understand what’s included. Rober, do you have a taxable investment account managed on an advisor platform?
Client 1: Yes—my taxable account on the advisor platform is 407,550.

Client 2: Yes. That private-markets one is 48,000.
Advisor: Thanks—helpful. Switching to the house for a moment, what do you use as the current value of your primary residence?
Client 2: The primary residence value is 305,000.
Advisor: Okay. Any loans against the house or anything else still outstanding?
Client 1: No loans outstanding. We don’t have a mortgage.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. Before we dive in, what made now the right time to come in?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. We’re trying to get a clean plan for the next few years—retire around 64, keep taxes sane, and make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like we’ve got a cushion and we understand what we actually have, not just a bunch of statements.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year right now, roughly?

Advisor: Great. And what state are you two residents of?
Client 2: WA.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so you know where I sit in all this—are we engaging through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage rep?
Client 1: Yes, it’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Got it. Now, earlier you both sounded pretty comfortable staying invested—how would you describe your overall risk tolerance in one word?

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. Before we dive in, what made now the right time to come in?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. We’re trying to get a clean plan for the next few years—retire around 64, keep taxes sane, and make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like we’ve got a cushion and we understand what we actually have, not just a bunch of statements.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year right now, roughly?
Client 1: It’s pension income, joint. The annual amount is 104800.

Client 1: Yes, it’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Got it. Now, earlier you both sounded pretty comfortable staying invested—how would you describe your overall risk tolerance in one word?
Client 2: I mean, I’m a little more cautious emotionally, but overall we’re aggressive.
Advisor: Okay, aggressive overall—thank you. While I’ve got you, I want to make sure I’ve touched every bucket at least once today: income, assets, liabilities, and protection.
Advisor: On income, you’ve got that pension coming in annually—104,800 a year—still the main income stream right now?

Client 1: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Thanks—super helpful. A couple more household basics, then I’ll stop peppering you with admin stuff.
Advisor: Jennifer, Rober—are you two married, or partnered some other way?
Client 2: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it.

Advisor: Taxes for a second—when you think about planning, what bracket do you usually assume you’re in? I know it can bounce around year to year.
Client 2: I always think we’re in the 25% bracket—
Client 1: No, Jen—35%. We’re in the 35% band.
Client 2: Right, sorry—35%.
Advisor: Great, thank you. That helps me frame things like Roth decisions and where dividends land.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like we’ve got a cushion and we understand what we actually have, not just a bunch of statements.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year right now, roughly?
Client 1: It’s pension income, joint. The annual amount is 104800.
Advisor: Thanks. And on the spending side, about what do you two spend per month all-in?
Client 2: We try to keep it steady. Total monthly expenses are 4000.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. Before we dive in, what made now the right time to come in?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. We’re trying to get a clean plan for the next few years—retire around 64, keep taxes sane, and make sure we’re not missing anything obvious.

Advisor: Okay—thanks for hanging with me, Rober and Jennifer. I want to zoom in on you two as individuals for a minute, just so the plan reflects who’s bringing in what.
Advisor: Rober, for your side, what do you use as your gross annual income number—before taxes—when you’re planning?
Client 1: For me personally, gross annual income is 81050.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And in the household, would you say you’re the primary person driving the finances and decisions, or more shared?
Client 1: I’m the primary.

Advisor: And you personally have a private-markets or alternative investment held through an insurance company?
Client 2: Yes. That private-markets one is 48,000.
Advisor: Thanks—helpful. Switching to the house for a moment, what do you use as the current value of your primary residence?
Client 2: The primary residence value is 305,000.
Advisor: Okay. Any loans against the house or anything else still outstanding?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And in the household, would you say you’re the primary person driving the finances and decisions, or more shared?
Client 1: I’m the primary.
Advisor: Okay. And work-wise, when you describe what you do day-to-day—are you more sales, tech, finance, operations… what bucket fits best?
Client 1: Operations, mostly.
Advisor: Perfect. One tiny admin thing, and then I’ll move on—when I label you in my notes, Rober, are you Client 1 or Client 2?
